videos.title: The ENTIRE History About Market Crash Due to COVID-19 (2020)
videos.text: do you remember the last time the world came to a standstill so dramatically that even the financial markets trembled the co9 pandemic triggered a crisis of historical proportions and what we witnessed was a devastating collapse in global markets but what was even more surprising was the impact an invisible virus had on entire economies forcing central banks to intervene with unprecedented measures if you want to understand what really happened behind the scenes of this extraordinary phenomenon and how it shaped the future of Finance get ready to uncover how this crash changed the rules of the game the start of an unprecedented crisis the covid-19 pandemic was a catalyst for one of the most significant crashes in global markets in recent memory at the start of 2020 as the virus spread alarmingly fast fear and uncertainty gripped investors the extraordinary nature of the situation was how quickly confidence in the global economic system crumbled leading to a historic Market collapse the financial markets which usually operate with some predictability began to experience abrupt daily fluctuations it was like watching a natural phenomenon of Destruction a wave of selling that seemed Unstoppable this created an atmosphere of curiosity mixed with fear about how deep this collapse would go Global markets always sensitive to news and forecasts began to react violently in investors worldwide started selling their assets seeking to protect themselves amid growing uncertainty the sharp drop in stock values of major companies became commonplace what was happening wasn't isolated it revealed how interconnected and influenced the global Financial system is by historical and unexpected events the collective intelligence of the markets which often gets predictions right fail to foresee the depth of this crisis catching even the most influential econom and investors off guard the situation worsened as constant updates about the rising covid-19 cases fueled panic in the stock market by February and March of 2020 when the pandemic was declared a global crisis Panic selling intensified it wasn't just an economic issue but a public health one which made markets even more volatile what began as a regional concern quickly spread globally in a devastating and Innovative way leading to Historic stock market crashes it was as if nature was reminding the world of its unpredictability showing that even the most sophisticated systems are vulnerable to its Mysteries a practical example of this impact was the collapse of the S&P 500 Index which fell over 30% within weeks influential companies once considered safe saw their values plummet and it wasn't just a stock market oil prices were also severely affected reaching historic lows this triggered a chain reaction sending many economic sectors into deep crisis to those watching from the outside it was hard to believe that all this was happening because of a microscopic virus but at the same time it revealed how fragile and interconnected the global economy had become as markets tumbled a growing curiosity emerged how would governments and central banks react the response came quickly and massive ly for the first time in modern history central banks from major world economies such as a Federal Reserve in the United States and the European Central Bank coordinated an unprecedented response they rolled out trillion doll stimulus packages slashed interest rates and took on a direct role in supporting economies in distress it was an extraordinary yet necessary measure to try to contain the devastating effects of the pandemic and bring some stability to Global financial markets this crisis also sparked an interesting reflection how would the world adapt to these new times The Accelerated digitalization remote work and changes in consumer patterns began to shape a new normal the nature of business changed drastically and many wondered if it would ever be possible to return to what it was before yet at the same time there was a growing awareness that the world was living through a historical moment something that would be studied for years as an example of human resilience and adaptation and even amid all the instability it became clear that after the storm New Opportunities would emerge the rapid spread of panic in the markets as the impact of covid-19 became apparent Panic spread as quickly as the virus itself investors driven largely by speculation and uncertainty began selling assets at an unprecedented speed what was extraordinary about this movement was how quickly confidence in the global economic system disintegrated sending the market into a downward spiral stock exchanges worldwide which typically operate with some predictability began to experience abrupt daily price swings it was like watching a natural disaster with a wave of selling that seemed Unstoppable this with fear as many wonder where this collapse would end this atmosphere of uncertainty was exacerbated by the fact that most investors had no idea how long the pandemic would last or what the real Economic Consequences would be without clear information the market began to operate on speculation which is the perfect breeding ground for panic many remembered similar historical events such as the 2008 crisis and started anticipating the worst the nature of the markets is often influenced by psychological phenomena and Collective fear fueled the crash even further interestingly unlike previous crises the trigger was not Financial but a virus something unexpected and Beyond the control of traditional economists during this time stocks of companies once considered safe began to plummet drastically technology companies transportation and tourism were the first to feel the impact with some experiencing declines of up to 40% in just weeks curiosity grew about how these companies would survive the storm and discussions about the economy's recovery dominated Financial conversations there was a stark contrast between businesses with adaptable models and those deeply exposed to the pandemic's effects intelligent investors began repositioning themselves seeking opportunities amid the chaos While others driven by Panic sold everything they owned the ripple effect didn't stopped there in addition to the stock market other sectors were severely affected the real estate market for instance slowed significantly as consumer confidence dropped and unemployment Rose demand for commercial real estate plummeted as companies Embrace remote work this was an Innovative and transformative moment as many realized that the way we work and live was changing permanently the pandemic aside from being a public health and economic disaster was also reshaping social and business paradigms the panic in the markets also led to to a series of governmental interventions central banks which previously hesitated to intervene on such a large scale were forced to adopt Extraordinary Measures the Federal Reserve in the United States for example cut interest rates to nearly zero and launch large-scale asset purchase programs these moves were designed to inject liquidity into the markets and restore investor confidence however the nature of this unprecedented intervention also sparked the debates about its long-term consequences would the economy become dependent on these stimulus measures this became a point of curiosity for many analysts as the Panic selling gradually subsided the damage was already done by March 2020 the market had lost trillions of dollars in value and Recovery seemed distant however amid the chaos some companies began to stand out particularly those in the technology online services sectors companies like Amazon zoom and Netflix saw significant increases in their stock prices as people increasingly relied on the internet for work communication and entertainment it was curious to see how while some sectors face collapse others thrived in the disaster the spread of panic in the markets was an extraordinary phenomenon but also served as a valuable lesson fear when combined with uncertainty can cause disproportionate reaction actions but it also opens doors to unexpected opportunities history has shown that in times of Crisis intelligence and long-term Vision are the greatest assets an investor can have even during one of the most significant crashes in history there were those who with courage and planning managed to navigate the turbulent Waters and find success the historic interventions of central banks faced with the gravity of the crisis central banks around the world had to act with speed and intensity never seen before the Federal Reserve the European Central Bank and other major economic players stepped in with Extraordinary Measures the market crash was so fast that without these interventions the consequences could have been even more devastating the extraordinary part of these actions was how they seemed to challenge traditional practices the sharp drop in interest rates the massive purchase of assets and the injection of trillions in liquidity into the financial system were seen as desperate yet intelligent attempts to stabilize the economy it showed that even in the most complicated situations Innovation can emerge from necessity the federal reserve's first major action was to cut interest rates to nearly zero which in itself was extraordinary never before in modern history had such a drastic rate cut been implemented so quickly additionally the FED announced large-scale ass asset purchases including government bonds and even corporate bonds this strategy known as quantitative easing was a clear example of how central banks were willing to do whatever it took to prevent the complete collapse of the financial system the Curiosity surrounding the situation was whether such measures would work in the long term or were just postponing the inevitable the European Central Bank ECB was also quit to act with an economy already weakened by years of low inflation and slow growth the Euro Zone fa a unique crisis to mitigate the pandemic's impact the ECB launched the pandemic emergency purchase program PPP a massive stimulus package aimed at ensuring European countries could continue to finance their expenses amid the crisis the nature of this program was Innovative and demonstrated the flexibility central banks needed to handle crises that at their core transcended economic boundaries the Curiosity here lay in how such an expansive program managed to maintain cohesion among countries with vastly different economic realities these interventions sparked an interesting debate about the independence of central banks some critics argued that by injecting so much money into the system these institutions were dangerously close to becoming permanent financiers of governments others believed it was the only viable solution to avoid a global depression the phenomenon unfolding was a challenge to traditional economic paradigms which have always preached fiscal and monetary Prudence however it was impossible to ignore that without these extraordinary actions the crisis could have led to an even deeper and more difficult to recover Global recession one of the most curious aspects of these interventions was how they affected risk perception in the market by lowering interest rates to Historic lows and purchasing large quantities of bonds central banks effectively push investors to seek riskier assets this created a situation where the stock market which had collapsed in March 2020 began to recover surprisingly quickly within months the S&P 500 which had lost 30% of its value was back to pre-crisis levels it was a historic recovery but it raised questions was this growth sustainable or were we witnessing a bubble inflated by artificial stimulus Beyond monetary policy fiscal interventions also played a crucial role in mitigating the economic damage of the pandemic governments worldwide launch trillion dollar stimulus packages offering direct support to businesses and citizens affected by the crisis in the United States the government passed the largest stimulus package in its history injecting trillions of dollars into the economy to support small businesses workers and struggling Industries this phenomenon of massive and coordinated intervention was unprecedented and questions about the long-term impact of these measures began to surface how would economies recover and what would be the final cost of these interventions ultimately the interventions of central banks and governments showed that in times of Crisis historic measures are necessary curiosity about the future of these economies persists especially regarding how these extraordinary actions will shape the coming years the extra ordinary aspect of this period is that it highlighted Humanity's ability to adapt and innovate in the face of colossal challenges yet at the same time it left us with a question what will the economic world look like post pandemic after interventions of such magnitude unprecedented volatility in stock markets of volatility that gripped stock markets during the 2020 crash was something unseen for decades it seemed like stock prices were on a roller coaster every day with swings so abrupt that even the most experienced investors were stunned the extraordinary nature of the situation was how the daily phenomena of the pandemic like Rising case numbers or new restrictions instantly influenced Market behavior in some moments news about vaccine development send stock soaring in others a new wave of infections would crash the indices again the Curiosity lay in the unpredictability with a sense that no one really knew what would happen next this volatility didn't just affect individual stocks broad indices like the S&P 500 the Dow Jones and the NASDAQ recorded daily movements of over 5% something uncommon in stable times the fear index known as the vix which measures expected Market volatility reached historic levels it was as if the markets had become Hostage to a cycle of uncertainty where any new information whether good or bad triggered intense reactions what was interesting was a change in investor Behavior with many becoming more defensive but also speculative driven by the fear of missing out on major opportunities or avoiding catastrophic losses the volatility was even more Curious when considering how different sectors reacted in opposite ways technology companies which were better positioned to adapt to remote work and the increasing demand for digital Solutions saw their stock sore Amazon for example nearly doubled in value within a few months while companies tied to tourism like Airlines and hotels fa devastating declines this contrast between winners and losers in the market created an interesting phenomenon of speculation where investors tried to identify the next companies that would thrive in an increasingly Digital World furthermore the nature of volatility in the oil Market was another defining feature of this crisis in April 2020 the price of US oil briefly traded in negative territory for the first time in history the world was perplexed by this extraordinary phenomenon which exposed the fragility of the Commodities Market amid a sudden drop in global demand the Curiosity about how markets would handle this new economic reality fueled intense discussions among experts after all it was something no one expected EX Supply combined with plummeting demand caused a Monumental price Distortion behind all this volatility artificial intelligence and algorithms played a significant role automated trading systems programmed to sell or buy large volumes of stocks based on rapid Market changes intensified the speed of declines and recoveries this led many to question whether human nature was being suppressed by machines in financial decision-making price movements became so unpredictable that even the most influential analysts struggled to provide consistent forecasts volatility had reached an Innovative level where the fusion of technology and financial markets created scenarios that seemed straight out of Science Fiction for retail investors volatility also represented an opportunity trading apps like Robin Hood saw an exponential increase in new users many of them drawn by the promise of making quick profits in such a dynamic Market the curiosity and fascination with riding the waves of volatility LED thousands of ordinary people to dive into the stock market buying shares of struggling companies or betting on a rapid recovery this Mass participation of individual investors driven by a mix of adrenaline and speculation added an unexpected component to Market Behavior something that would be studied for years to come the unprecedented volatility of 2020 showed how extraordinarily sensitive financial markets can be to unpredictable Global events but at the same time it highlighted the resilience and adaptability of the economic system in the end despite all the turbulence the markets began to find some equilibrium however the lesson for investors is clear in times of Crisis the only certainty is uncertainty and learning to navigate these turbulent Waters requires emotional intelligence strategic vision and above all a constant curiosity about the behavior of the global economy the impact on oil and commodities prices the crash of 2020 didn't only affect stock markets the commodity's market especially oil was severely hit by the crisis caused by the covid-19 pandemic the drop in global demand for fuel driven by travel restrictions and Industrial shutdowns led to an extraordinary phenomenon oil prices plummeted into negative territory in April 201 20 this historic event shock many sparking massive curiosity about what it meant to trade oil at a negative price simply put the cost of storing oil exceeded its sale price as excess Supply and lack of demand caused an unprecedented collapse in prices the impact was felt worldwide oil exporting countries like Saudi Arabia and Russia faced enormous challenges balancing their economies as their revenues plummeted overnight Futures contracts on oil a widely used Financial instrument were also heavily impacted causing panic among investors the extraordinary aspect of the situation was how such a vital natural resource became temporarily a burden for those who owned it the nature of global oil demand had changed radically in just a few weeks and the market was unprepared for this turn of events other Commodities such as gold and silver experienced wild fluctuations however unlike oil which crashed gold traditionally seen as a safe haven in times of Crisis saw a significant increase in its value in moments of extreme uncertainty investors turned to gold as a protective asset pushing its price to record highs this highlighted how in turbulent times some assets can behave inversely to others creating a series of opportunities for a student investors the phenomenon of Safe Haven assets wasn't new but the intensity of the rush to Gold during the pandemic was impressive Beyond oil and gold other Commodities like food and Industrial Metals also faced unexpected fluctuations the global supply chain was disrupted affecting everything from agricultural production to car manufacturing demand for basic food items surged due to panic buying in various parts of the world driving up prices for products like rice and wheat on the other hand Commodities like copper and aluminum essential for industries that halted during the pandemic saw their prices plummet the Curiosity here lay in how the pandemic affected the economy in such distinct ways depending on the sector and commodity in question as Global economies gradually began to recover the Commodities Market started to adjust but the scars remained the 2020 crisis taught the world how the balance between supply and demand could be abruptly broken in the case of oil a coordinated effort between OPEC countries and other producing Nations was necessary to reduce Supply and stabilize prices this agreement was a clear example of how in times of Crisis International cooperation can be essential to avoid even greater economic disasters the nature of these negotiations was strategic showing that even amid a Health crisis the geopolitics of oil continued to play an influential role in the global economy lastly the crisis in the Commodities Market brought several important lessons it highlighted the importance of diversification for both investors and countries heavily riing on a single resource the Curiosity about how markets will evolve in the future particularly with the transition to renewable energy became one of the main points of debate after all if a phenomenon like the pandemic could make oil worth less than nothing what will happen when dependency on fossil fuels begins to decline with the energy transition this question became one of the most important for those watching the future of Commodities and the global economy the rise of big Tech amid the chaos while many economic sectors struggled during the 2020 crisis A specific group of companies saw an extraordinary opportunity for growth the tech Giants the pandemic radically changed people's behavior forcing them to rely more on digital solutions for work communication and entertainment the phenomenon of remote work for example made tools like Zoom Microsoft teams and Google meet essential in the daily lives of millions of people worldwide the Curiosity here was how the nature of these companies with their innovative business models allowed them to adapt and thrive in such an adverse environment companies like Amazon Apple Facebook and Google alphabet became even more influential during this period the demand for online services and e-commerce skyrocketed as people avoided physical stor stores and embraced online shopping Amazon in particular saw its stock price rise dramatically as consumers increasingly depended on its platform for essential Goods it was extraordinary to see how amid the chaos big tech companies were able to expand their businesses while other sectors like traditional retail face bankruptcies and massive Revenue declines this contrast sparked growing curiosity about the capacity of these companies to shaped the future of the global economy another fascinating phenomenon was a rise of Digital entertainment platforms like Netflix YouTube and the newly launched Disney plus with millions of people stuck at home due to quarantines and social isolation demand for digital content soared Netflix for instance saw a record increase in subscribers further solidifying its position as one of the largest entertainment companies in the world this demonstrated how the nature of entertainment was changing with digital platforms replacing traditional media big Tech which had already revolutionize how we consume media now stood as the leaders of a new normal in addition to entertainment the tech sector was crucial in transforming entire Industries education for example underwent a drastic change with online classes being conducted on a massive scale platforms like Google classroom and Microsoft teams became essential Tools for Schools and universities worldwide the Curiosity here was how education an area that had historically been slow to adopt technological changes suddenly became entirely dependent on these Solutions this sparked a new conversation about the role of technology and education and whether these changes would become permanent after the pandemic ended in the financial sector big Tech also began to leave a more visible Mark fintech companies like PayPal and square experi significant growth as consumers moved to digital payments and avoided using physical cash due to contamination fears the nature of this growth was Innovative as these companies not only facilitated online transactions but also expanded their services to include credit Investments and even cryptocurrencies this opened up new curiosity about how the future of Finance would be shaped by tech companies which were now directly competing with traditional banks in many areas meanwhile The Growing Power and presence of these Tech Giants began to attract criticism and concern the increase in the influence of big Tech during the pandemic led many to question whether these corporations were becoming too large to control discussions about monopolies data privacy and regulation began to gain traction particularly with the increasing use of social media platforms and the mass collection of personal information the Curiosity surrounding how governments would address these issues balancing Innovation with protecting citizens rights became one of the most intense debates of 2020 ultimately the rise of big Tech during the 2020 crash showed how technological innovation and intelligence can Thrive even in the most challenging times these companies already influential now position themselves as fundamental pillars of the new digital economy and the remaining curiosity is how far can they go will we see new companies emerge to compete with these Giants or are we heading toward a future where a few tech companies control vast areas of the global economy changes in consumer Behavior the 2020 crisis brought profound changes in consumer Behavior directly impacting how people shop work and interact with social isolation measures and government restrictions imposed to contain the spread of covid-19 routines were transformed and the extraordinary became the new normal the Curiosity about how people would adapt to this new reality was a major point of debate among economists and experts the most obvious phenomenon was the acceleration of digitalization with consumers massively migrating to e-commerce streaming platforms and delivery services the nature of consumption was changing and companies had to adjust quickly to meet this new demand one of the most striking changes was the explosion of online shopping platforms like Amazon Marcato Libra and Alibaba saw a significant increase in users and transactions products that were traditionally purchased in physical stores such as food clothing and electronics were now being bought online the phenomenon of fast deliveries also intensified with consumers demanding convenience and safety when receiving products at home this not only transformed how people shopped but also had a direct impact on traditional retail which faced one of its greatest crises in history many physical stores Clos their doors While others rushed to transition to digital to survive another significant change was in the consumption of entertainment and media time spent at home LED to an extraordinary Surge and demand for streaming services such as Netflix Amazon Prime video and Disney plus online gaming platforms and social media also saw usage spikes as people sought ways to connect and entertain themselves during isolation this phenomenon of accelerated digital consumption showed how technology could meet emotional and psychological needs creating growing curiosity about what the future technology holds for entertainment and social interaction food consumption habits also change notably during the crisis the home cooking phenomenon gain momentum driven by restaurant closures and the need to save money food delivery services like Uber Eats door Dash and GrubHub saw significant growth but there was also a notable increase in the search for online recipes and interest in cooking more elaborate dishes at home people suddenly became more curious about their culinary skills and began dedicating more time to meal preparation this created a new market for products aimed at this audience such as meal kits and Innovative kitchen utensils the changes in work patterns were one of the most Innovative and significant Transformations the remote work phenomenon previously restricted to certain companies became the norm for millions of people worldwide technology financial services and even the public sector Embrace remote work on a large scale realizing that productivity could be maintained and in some cases even increased outside the traditional office environment the Curiosity that followed was about the future of offices would remote work remain after the crisis transforming the nature of offices and professional interactions the psychological impact of the pandemic also altered how consumers perceive the value of certain products and services with economic uncertainties and concerns about health many began prioritizing purchases that ensured safety and well-being such as hygiene products and health related items at the same time there was a drop in the consumption of Superfluous Goods as people began to reflect more on their purchasing priorities this change in mindset sparked curiosity among businesses about how to cater to a consumer who after the pandemic would likely be more cautious selective and interested in products offering real value and utility finally changes in consumer Behavior brought an important reflection on human adaptation in the face of crises the extraordinary nature of this transformation wasn't just the speed at which it occurred but also the resilience and creativity people demonstrated in times of uncertainty the Curiosity about what comes next how new consumption Trends will develop from these changes remains alive and companies that can anticipate these new demands will be well positioned to thrive in an uncertain but opportunity filled future the role of fintex and the cryptocurrency boom during the 2020 crisis fintex and cryptocurrencies played an extraordinary role in how people manage their finances the pandemic accelerated the digitalization of many aspects of life and the financial sector was no exception the use of digital payments surged as consumers avoided physical cash for health reasons and fexs like PayPal new bank and square were able to meet this growing demand what was curious was how these platforms already Innovative by Nature became essential in a world that was rapidly transforming they allowed individuals and businesses to conduct transactions safely and quickly at a time when physical Mobility was severely restricted the p pandemic also brought a shift in how people thought about Investments and cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin took Center Stage amid economic instability and the volatility of traditional currencies many began to see cryptocurrencies as an intriguing alternative Bitcoin which had long sparked curiosity saw its value Skyrocket in 2020 as investors sought a store of value outside the traditional Financial system the phenomenon of cryptocurrencies wasn't just a resp response to the crisis but also a signal that the financial world was becoming more decentralized and digital this shift raised questions about the future of Fiat currencies and the role of central banks in a world where digital money is gaining momentum the extraordinary growth of finex also showed how access to credit and banking services could be democratized companies like new bank revet and chime which offer digital banking services without exorbitant fees and with a userfriendly interface gain millions of new customers during the pandemic at a time when many people face financial hardship finex offered quick and accessible Solutions such as flexible lines of credit and payment facilities this Innovative Financial phenomenon was especially important in regions where access to traditional banking services is limited showing how Financial technology can transform lives and promote Financial inclusion on a global scale Beyond crypto currencies another Innovative Financial phenomenon that gained momentum during the crisis was the increasing use of blockchain technology to ensure transparency and Security in digital transactions blockchain the underlying technology of Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies began to be applied in other sectors such as Logistics and Supply chains to track products and ensure the authenticity of goods the Curiosity surrounding how blockchain could revolutionize the economy grew as the pandemic exposed the fragility of Global Supply chains many companies began exploring this technology as a solution to challenges related to traceability and Security in an increasingly Digital World the crisis also highlighted the importance of finex in crowdfunding and micro Finance platforms like Kickstarter and GoFundMe saw significant increase in the number of campaigns as small businesses and individuals sought alternatives to Finance their projects or simply survived during the pandemic this financial Innovation empowered individuals allowing them to seek funding directly from their communities or a global audience the crowdfunding phenomenon which was already popular in specific niches became a real alternative to Traditional Bank financing at a time when credit was harder to obtain finally the cryptocurrency boom and the role of fintex during the 2020 crash rais many questions about the future of the Global Financial system are we heading toward a fully digital economy where traditional banks will have less relevance could cryptocurrencies one day replace conventional money the Curiosity about these issues is great and what became clear during the crisis is that Financial technology is moving in an Innovative and irreversible Direction those who embrac this change have the chance to be part of a more Dynamic inclusive and decentralized financial future the Imp on the job market and the future wor the 2020 crisis brought an extraordinary shift in the job market social distancing and restrictive measures imposed by governments to contain the pandemic forc companies across all sectors to rethink their operational models one of the most notable phenomena was the mass adoption of remote work something that before the pandemic was considered a privilege for a few suddenly millions of people around the world were working from home and the nature of the workplace changed dramatically the Curiosity that arose was about the sustainability of this model in the long run and whether even after the crisis remote work would become a permanent part of professional life the adaptation to remote work raised questions about productivity and the balance between personal and professional life while many workers adjusted well enjoying the flexibility that working from home offers others struggled especially those who had to balance family and work responsibilities in the same space Additionally the lack of face-to-face interactions led many to feel isolated highlighting the emotional challenges of working from home this phenomenon brought renewed curiosity about the future of traditional offices influential companies like Twitter and Facebook announc that remote work could become permanent for their employees sparking a debate about the future of in-person work at the same time the pandemic accelerated Automation in various sectors with companies trying to minimize the risk and cost associated with hiring human labor many began investing in artificial intelligence and process Automation in the industrial sector for example robots replace workers in several functions and even service sectors such as customer support began adopting chatbots and automated systems this extraordinary phenomenon raises questions about the future of employment in a world increasingly driven by automation will workers be replaced by machines on a large scale curiosity about the professions of the future became Central to discussions about the impact of the crisis on the job market another important aspect was the rise of the gig and freelance economy as a solution for many who lost their jobs during the pandemic platforms like uber F and upwork saw a significant increase in the number of workers seeking temporary or Project based work the flexible nature of this economy allowed people to adapt to the New Economic reality but it also raised concerns about the precariousness of these jobs and the lack of traditional benefits such as health insurance and retirement the growth phenomenon of this alternative economy highlighted the need to rethink labor Protections in a world where permanent employment may become increasingly rare additionally the impact of the pandemic on sectors such as tourism Aviation and events was devastating millions of workers in these sectors lost their jobs or had their contract suspended hotels Airlines restaurants and entertainment companies were some of the hardest hit and many workers are still struggling to find viable Alternatives however other sectors like technology Logistics and Healthcare began to demand more workers especially for roles related to delivery ecomerce and medical care this contrast sparked curiosity about how worker skills need to evolve to align with the demands of a rapidly transforming Market the phenomenon of reskilling and upskilling also gained prominence during the crisis as many workers realized their skills were becoming obsolete there was a rush for online courses and training to acquire new competencies particularly in technology and data analysis platforms like corera udemy and Linkedin learning saw an exponential increase in enrollment as people sought ways to reinvent themselves professionally curiosity about the future of work also fueled this pursuit of new knowledge with the perception that the professions of the future would require more specialized and digital skills the 2020 crisis forever altered the job market curiosity about what the future of employment will look like where automation remote work and the gig economy coexist will continue to be a topic of great interest in the coming years while some see these changes as threats others view them as extraordinary opportunities for Innovation and personal growth at the heart of this transformation is adaptation those who can keep up with the evolution of Market demands and Trends will be ready to thrive in a future that is increasingly unpredictable the emergence of new investment opportunities amid the chaos of the 2020 crisis new investment opportunities began to emerge often in areas few expected the market volatility which initially triggered panic among investors ended up opening doors for those who could identify Innovative Trends one of the most curious phenomena was the increased interest in sectors related to technology health care and sustainability companies involved in vaccine development hygiene products and medical equipment saw their stock sore while Tech startups emerg with solutions for remote work and business digitalization this scenario reinforced the idea that even in times of Crisis the intelligence of knowing knowing where to invest can generate extraordinary Returns the technology sector already one of the most influential before the pandemic became one of the biggest beneficiaries of this movement investors realized that companies facilitating the transition to the digital world such as video conferencing platforms e-commerce and cloud computing were not only weathering the crisis but thriving curiosity grew about how these companies would continue to expand after the pandemic and many began to see them as the future of global business the phenomenon of digital transformation opened opportunities for investors who were attentive to changing consumer Behavior and the growing role of technology in the economy another sector that caught investors attention was renewable energy with the crisis many countries began to rethink their energy policies and the demand for Sustainable Solutions increased significantly investments in solar wind and electric vehicle companies began to gain popularity driven by both the need for economic diversification and government incentives Tesla for instance became one of the most valuable companies in the world during this period with investors curious about how the transition to clean energy would shape the future of Transportation the YG environmental social and governance phenomenon also gained traction with investors seeking companies committed to sustainable and responsible practices fintex which were already already on the rise before the crisis became another focus of investment opportunities with the increasing demand for digital payments and growing distrust in traditional banking systems fex continued to attract Venture Capital companies like stripe and square saw their valuations rise rapidly as more consumers and small businesses adopted digital Financial Services Additionally the cryptocurrency boom led by Bitcoin sparked a new wave of investors curious to explore this decentralized world the nature of this Market which challenged conventional Financial models created both opportunities and uncertainties the pandemic also spurred a new wave of investments in the biotechnology and healthc Care sectors companies racing to develop a covid-19 vaccine such as fiser madna and biente saw a massive influx of capital as investors bet on a quick solution to the crisis additionally startups focus on Health Technologies such as tele medicine and remote Diagnostics began to emerge with Force the Curiosity surrounding how biotechnology would continue to evolve after the pandemic became one of the major topics of debate among investors the crisis's impact accelerated innovation in this sector creating extraordinary opportunities for those willing to invest in long-term Solutions finally another interesting phenomenon was the Resurgence of interest in the real estate market particularly outside major Urban centers with remote work becoming a permanent trend for many companies demand for properties in suburban and rural areas increased creating new opportunities for Real Estate Investors people who previously depended on being close to their offices could now move to more distant regions where the cost of living was lower and the quality of life often better this phenomenon sparked curiosity about how the real estate market would transform with a possible decentralization of major cities and a new Dynamic of supply and demand the investment opportunities that emerged in 2020 showed that even amid a historic crisis Innovation and strategic intelligence can open doors to financial success the Curious part is that many of the sectors that prospered were once considered risky bets before the pandemic but the nature of the market is like this unpredictable extraordinary and full of surprise is for those who know where to look as the world begins to recover the Lessons Learned in 2020 will continue to guide investors in their future decisions Always In Search of the next big opportunity now that you've learned how the market crash of 2020 unfolded what are you going to do with this knowledge the world of Finance is ever changing and this crisis proved that adaptability and intelligence are key to navigating uncertain times are you ready to take control of your financial future let these lessons inspire you to rethink your strategies and explore new opportunities don't forget to subscribe to the channel and activate notifications to keep learning more about the extraordinary world of Finance

videos.title: Stock Market Crash March 2020 (what to do?)
videos.text: hello everyone welcome to another episode of owl investing and in this episode I'm going to be talking about the stock market crash of 2020 how I'm navigating through and how you can as well so don't forget to subscribe and let's get right into the video [Music] we are in March 2020 right now and as I speak we are having one of the worst stock market crashes that we've had in recent history and my heart goes out to everyone out there that's suffering major losses but more importantly I want to send out my condolences to friends and families of people that have suffered from the coronavirus so far the SMP 500 has lost about 18% Italy is shut down fully quarantined as a country nobody is allowed to leave their homes and Saudi Arabia and Russia failed to reach an agreement which led to the decline of oil prices by 20% now that's a pretty bad combination of events that led to most people individual stock investors like you and I go out and sell everything that they have people are freaking out and everybody's going out there and selling their stock portfolios and nobody wants to be in the game as a focus dividend investor I never lose track of my endgame I invest for dividend income and a retirement focused portfolio I want to retire off dividends in the future and I want to own a few of the best quality companies in the world so when a crash like this happens I see this as an opportunity to buy these best quality companies at a major discount since the beginning of the history of the stock market stock market crashes have 100% of the time bounced back some will take one year others will take five years at the end of the day all of them bounce back and because I'm a long-term investor I see this market dip as a chance for me to buy more stocks and increase my returns when the market bounces back but the hard part of this all is resisting the urge to sell your stocks it's hard for everyone out there and it's hard for me as well but when that thought passes through my brain I repeat the mantra keep the endgame in mind just repeat that in your head say it in front of mirror over and over again and it will boost your confidence to do the right thing so what do you do now that the market has crashed do you try to find new companies to invest in or do you invest in companies that you already own the answer is it depends everybody out there has a different in a portfolio and there is no one answer fits all kind of thing each portfolio is catered to their own holders and the best thing that I can do for you is tell you what I'm doing with my portfolio and hopefully you can learn something that you can apply in your own situation for me I only invest in a few number of companies 15 at max because research shows that a portfolio with a small amount of companies has a higher chance of beating the sp500 returns over the long period of time I keep the number of fifteen as my max limit but I also focus the majority of my holdings in my top 10 positions right now currently I have 14 stocks in my portfolio leaving that one empty position for when a really really good opportunity comes along as of right now I don't see this opportunity happening unless the market drops to 25 to 30 percent at least so until then I'm not going to be going out there trying to find new companies to invest in because I'm just going to be adding to my current positions that I have and especially I'm going to be adding to my top ten core positions that I currently own because I plan to hold these stocks for a very long time hopefully forever I have done a lot of research on the companies that I currently own which led me to buying them in the first place the fundamentals and the reasons behind why I bought these companies haven't changed so a market crash shouldn't change my position in the matter the companies are just now selling at a lower price so of course I'll be buying more I love buying these companies at lower prices because I truly believe in them I believe in their long-term prospects and I believe in their sustainability and I believe in them to help me achieve my financial goals in the future so as of right now I started buying these companies and I started buying them at a more aggressive pace and by aggressive pace I don't mean spending all of my money in one week but instead of buying stocks once a month I am buying them twice a month and I'm increasing the dollar amount that I'm buying with because frankly I don't know how long this crash is gonna last it could last two months or even a year but looking at where the prices are right now I'm comfortable buying at a more aggressive pace because I know these are cheap prices for the companies that I own now in the case of oil our memory is still fresh from the 2015 oil recession that happened and what happened is a lot of small oil companies have filed for bankruptcy and a lot of the bigger oil companies have survived but are still suffering from that recession till this date I'm staying away from oil right now because I believe oil companies tend to take a long time for their prices to adjust to current market conditions and usually it takes about three months for the price to fully sink in with the events that are happening right now so my advice for you is wait about three months until you want to make any decision on an oil company that you want to buy I've learned that the hard way in the 2015 recession where I started buying too soon and three months after I bought the prices were way better than what I bought a mat so I've learned my lesson and I'm going to wait a little bit for oil however I'm not really excited about the future prospects of oil I got into oil heavily back in the 2015 recession because I thought there were some great value stocks back then however as time went on and I grew as an investor and I learned more I prefer to stay in businesses that are not commodity businesses businesses that have franchises and that are monopolies or duopoly these type of businesses provide healthier profit margins better business fundamentals in general so yes I am invested in oil but I'm not that excited about it I won't sell my oil stock position but I'm not going to be adding a lot to it either even to be months if the price goes lower I'm just gonna add a small amount I'm gonna definitely focus on my top ten positions so that I can get those up to a level where I want them to be so that's what I'm doing with my oil company that I own right now which is Suncor by the way a ticker symbol su I'll give you guys an example of one of the companies that are in my core positions that I'm currently buying right now which is the company 3m I started buying 3m at the end of last year I believed it was a great company back then and I still believe it's a great company right now the company fundamentals haven't changed in fact I'll put a link in description below if you guys want to check that out I did an analysis video on 3m it's very informative has a lot of information on 3m so if you guys want to check it out click the link in the description and yes 3m might get affected by the corona virus but like any other pandemic in history they all pass and when it passes 3m is still going to be a great company own over the long run and you will be able to capitalize on the current fear of the market it's hard for me as it is hard for everyone out there to keep their composure in times like these it's a lot easier to keep your composure when you own a group of great quality companies and I'm a lot more comfortable seeing those top quality companies go down then I'm comfortable seeing poor average companies with unhealthy balance sheets go down and that's another advantage of limiting your stock portfolio to 15 stocks because it forces you to pick very very carefully right now I'm happy to see all my companies go down because I'll happily buy more and more of these companies and never have to worry about selling them at any point of my life this is the mentality you have to have when buying any company because you're not going to be able to find the best time to buy or sell a stock so the best decision for you as an individual investor is to plan to buy for life by doing that you're decreasing the risk that you take and in 30 years who cares if you bought 3m at 150 or 160 it really doesn't matter it's gonna mean nothing to you at that point companies that are gonna suffer the most right now are companies that have poor return on equities large amount of debt compared to their equities and low profit margins so definitely stay away from those companies and again focus on your top quality companies even if they haven't gone down as much as the other companies have focus on the fundamentals that's gonna help you out the most focus investing is hard and not for everyone if you're just starting out I highly recommend that you invest in an index fund that tracks the S&P 500 or the Nasdaq index and keep investing money in the sp500 right now and you'll be just fine however if you want to achieve better than average results then focus investing is definitely the way to do it I chose focus investing because I do want to achieve better than average results but I also want to control what I own I also do it because of ethical reasons partially due to the fact that I'm RELIGIOUS and I don't want to buy companies that sell cigarettes or alcohol or sell guns and weapons that are going to harm the community and the world that we live in as a whole I want to feel great about the companies that I own and I want to pass that mentality to my kids and grandkids hopefully one day so whether I beat the market in the long run or don't it doesn't really matter to me because that's what I chose to do what matters to me the most is at the end of my days people around me are going to say that I cared more about my values putting in the work and not about the results so to summarize it all number one do not panic and do not sell hold on number two buy more of your core stocks the companies that have the highest quality and I'll leave you all with a quote from the great Warren Buffett be fearful when others are greedy and be greedy when others are fearful that's all I have for you guys today make sure to like this video and subscribe to my channel and share in the comments below what you guys are doing in this market crash I'd love to hear your opinions and we can talk about it check out my other videos as well thank you and I'll see you in the next investing video

videos.title: The Fed understands the severity of the coronavirus: Lazard director
videos.text: what's important for viewers to understand this morning in your view well I think there are a few things one is we still don't know how many people are truly infected with kovat 19 I guess the latest numbers from Johns Hopkins suggest almost 3,800 when we look at it I believe that we're likely to find the true number is somewhere between 10 and 20,000 at this point seems like a reasonable number and when I think about this kind of extrapolating out the key key point I'm focused on is where are the vulnerabilities in the in the healthcare system in the United States and if we assume a doubling rate which is basically a kind of fancy way of saying how many days does it take for the number of cases to double of around six days that means we could be at 434 thousand cases within say 33 days if you're starting at ten thousand or twenty seven days if you're starting with twenty thousand the reason this is an important number is the US based on our analysis has about twenty 1,700 intensive care unit beds available or that could be called into service if we typically find about fifteen percent of people with kovat 19 need hospitalization and one out of three of those people need an intensive care unit bed at four hundred thirty four thousand units we could be out of ICU bed capacity so I think importantly by the way the beds aren't special what's important is that you have a ventilator because these are people in respiratory distress and you also need health care experts who are trained to deal with these people so so that's the most important point we're looking at on the healthcare side right now yeah your your language is striking in your note here Ron and I'll quote while the Trump administration has either not grasped the severity of the situation or has not been honest about it to date the Fed does appreciate the gravity of the disruption to the economy and markets I guess on both fronts a White House response and now fed what's your what's your grade well let's start with the Fed I would give the Fed an A I mean I think the Fed recognized they did not have a lot of dry powder to work with they also realized that if you don't have a lot to work with you're better off trying to achieve a bit of a shock and ought to get you must most bang for your buck and what they announced yesterday I think was a pretty sweeping attack on the problem and they're doing all they can do so I give the Fed an A and we can dig into that if you like more but the Fed can't solve the coronavirus and that really will the federal government to basically be at the front on the front foot and what's disappointing to me so far is frankly even the bipartisan compromise legislation they passed the house on Friday night has a giant loophole if you know one of the key intentions of the legislation was to make sure that people who are sick don't go to work and infect other people but the legislation allows exemptions for any company with over 500 employees and allows for an application for hardship exemptions for any company with under 50 employees well that's 80 percent of Americans work in those companies which effectively means you're making people choose between putting food on the table or potentially spreading an infection and if I look at the press conference on Friday at 3:30 from the White House it was disappointing there as it quickly became apparent that the the website for a national database that supposedly was being worked on by Google wasn't accurately portrayed and also you know it's great that these companies are offering capacity and they're parking lots for testing but it also still isn't clear who's going to be responsible to roll out that testing and when it will be available so so I think what we realize is the federal government has either been not aware of the situation or has been too focused on politics to make people aware and this is really important for people to understand the importance of social distancing they have to be told the truth and they have to understand these issues around ICU capacity and effectiveness and I worry that we've had two months that have largely been wasted in terms of communicating that message yeah Ron I mean certainly there's a lot of different factors that are playing into all of this right now and we know from Peter Navarro on our air earlier today that you're gonna start to see some executive orders draft that some of those things like medical supplies for example coming back to be manufactured here in the US as well so we're gonna have to see how all of that continues to manifest in the meantime I want to get your thoughts on the market specifically the fact that we're seeing I guess I should say the behavior that we're seeing Manifesta across different asset classes right now the fact that you would have equity is selling off but also gold selling off here I mean the Treasury market specifically has been used as a gauge or a signifier for behavior or correlations across other assets and we've seen that essentially break down in the past week as well do you feel like there has been I guess a disentanglement or that that relationship has been compromised I think what you're seeing in markets is actually it's a combination of factors and I guess point number one would be I think it would be unwise to try to trade this you know because you can obviously get whipsawed and lose a lot of money but I think what we saw last week in particular with Treasuries selling off at the same time as equities was partly for Steve risking or basically deleveraging of some levered investors who were having to sell their liquid assets to try to meet collateral calls and basically because in many cases risk models would force them to be leveraged as volatility goes up so to your point historically you would be rebalancing from one asset class to the other but in this case volatility went up and in all asset classes and forced people to lower their risk levels so so I do think that was hopefully not a long-term anomaly but you know I do think it's a great example we've seen this in Prior crises by the way or in Prior sharp downturns that we always have these mathematical models that tell us what correlation should be and then whenever things are really bad correlations go to one they don't end up being negative yeah and Ron to your point about the Fed and just the federal government in general right now how worrisome is the credit market picture and what do you think needs to actually happen to keep credit markets open so I I do worry that as we see these announcements rolling out for example last night from Illinois and Ohio New York City's announcement in terms of basically shutting down bars and restaurants or avoiding congregations of over 50 people I do think this is incredibly painful for the small businesses in the u.s. so so basically I think there's a critical need from Washington to figure out a way to a have a bulletproof sick leave policy not what we passed on Friday but something much more robust be to make sure that there are ways for companies to get access to liquidity and maybe it might even end up being government grants or aid to keep people employed because again the most important thing right now is for people to engage in social distancing if people are sick they should stay away from work and make sure they don't infect other people and we need to make sure that we keep that money flowing so people don't have to choose between paying the mortgage going to work yeah I I think that's part one but then part two on the credit markets the Fed does still have more tools there are limits and let the fake can do but I think what they announced last night is very powerful and should get money flowing back into these markets I'm a little surprised mortgages aren't responding more positively I think the mortgage part of the announcement was very important but keep in mind the Fed had many programs innovate oh nine that they use to try to make sure credit money got where it needed to go in the economy to keep credit markets open you

the coronavirus would be during closed-door senate briefings was sell a huge amount of stock right before the market tanked senate intel chairman richard burr sold up to 1.7 million dollars worth senator dianne feinstein sold up to six million dollars worth senator james inhofe sold up to 400 grand and senator kelly loffler dumped up to 3 million bucks and bought shares of oracle and citrix you know that does video conferencing used to support remote work meanwhile here's what they were doing publicly some of these very same senators who knew what was happening and acting accordingly privately making money you know what they were publicly upbeat and cheerful all about the outlook new at six both of georgia's u.s senators are under fire for allegedly trying to profit from the coronavirus pandemic records show that senators kelly lefler and david perdue bought and sold stock shortly after a private senate briefing on the virus this is an unbelievably historic week for the fed and it did more this week than it's ever done before and it's going to do more today i believe than it's ever done before and there are calls for it to do much much more let's look at what's going to happen today fed plants 107 billion dollars worth of purchases in a single day that breaks down into 75 billion dollars of treasuries and 32 billion dollars of mortgages that's a market that the north carolina burr has no background in finance or investing in fact before being elected to congress in 1994 burr spent 17 years selling lawn mowers for a dealer in winston-salem but on february 13th of this year burst seemed to develop very strong feelings about the future of emeriti american equity markets that day he dumped up to 1.7 million dollars in stocks in 33 separate transactions we're going to start with some positive news stock futures are rallying across the board nasdaq futures up better than two percent s p endow futures up about two percent right now and that's because ackman speaking with scott wapner on cnbc back on march 18th that appearance as you may remember has stoked a lot of controversy after it was revealed that ackman's pershing square netted a 2.6 billion gain from a bet against the equity markets been a historic week and historic week on wall street from fed actions to a big stimulus package in washington to the dow's biggest three-day rally that the senator provided documents to the fbi okay we are going to start this hour with the strength of the u.s economy fed chairman jay powell telling 60 minutes last night not to bet against america but he added that the recovery will likely be long and drawn out i'm surprised at how quickly the market has come back however we have a market that wants to go up we are as a people and i hate to get theoretical on this but it's a little bit it's okay every once in a while people do want to see things better and i think whether it's the analysts whether it's companies expectations were very very low if they were given at all and i think the fact that we've seen a little bit of a glimmer of hope these markets wanted to see that and i think we're going to continue to see that rise as we move forward if people can put masks on we might come out of this even faster on the uh the economic front but right now the markets are looking 12 months ahead and they're saying we do see high of 520 a share it's not just about stocks it's about the curve steepening uh it's about drucker miller talking about uh breath broadening and a lot more yeah this is one of those uh rallies that it's very interesting because uh stan drucklemer talked about marty's why and his views of the 1980s they're pertinent as ever we've got some incredible breadth uh we have a fed that is completely on airlines we had some numbers from phil lebeau uh off a very low basis talking about flying it's almost as if people decided covet's over uh it's a v-shaped rally and you better get on board
